original_abstract: Distributional semantic models derive computational representations of word meaning by analyzing patterns of lexical co-occurrence within large text corpora. While these models have become a cornerstone of computational linguistics—offering reliable estimates of semantic relatedness across various processing tasks—they rely exclusively on linguistic input. This reliance on text constitutes a significantly impoverished foundation compared to the multisensory perceptual streams that ground human semantic knowledge. To address this lack of grounding, we leverage computer vision techniques that automatically identify discrete "visual words" within images. By doing so, we extend the distributional representation of a concept to encompass its co-occurrence with visual features in its associated imagery. We propose a flexible multimodal architecture designed to integrate textual and visual distributional information. Our empirical evaluations demonstrate that this integrated model consistently outperforms purely text-based approaches, capturing complementary semantic nuances that are absent from linguistic data alone.
humanized_abstract: A growing body of work derives computational meaning representations for words by exploring patterns of lexical co-occurrence that they may embed within large corpora of text, and using these representations to compute word meaning in a number of computational linguistic tasks accurately; particularly those involving word semantics and semantic relationship estimation. In these models words are strictly used linguistically and semantically meaning is hence derived solely from text. Although many text features are obviously more adequate for linguistic purpose than the crude multi-modal percept that underpins all our own personal knowledge about semantics and meaning this basis is even more inadequate than it is for most uses of contemporary language processing algorithms than, for example, speech and even when speech is the input, a surprisingly large number of linguistic artefacts have to be removed or replaced by more natural or direct sensory representations, in order to permit interpretation as spoken rather than written. To help to redress this lack of multisensory ground-ing for purely linguistic approaches we derive from computer vision the set of visual words which each image is associated with; we then map concepts for which there is sufficient training text into the set of visual words that appear within all the images in which they appear. We describe the framework for doing this multimodally as well as purely based upon text and present a number of results that demonstrate a surprisingly significant, although typically around a per cent or so increase in precision over the baseline text-based algorithms in estimating many forms of the typical computational semantic similarity measures associated with text-based models of meaning.
